title: What Is Docker?
description:
## Containers, Images and the Docker Engine

**Docker** packages an application together with its dependencies into a portable unit called a **container**. Containers share the host kernel (unlike virtual machines, which each run a full OS), so they start in milliseconds and are lightweight.

### The three core concepts

| Term | What it is |
|---|---|
| **Image** | A read-only template — a snapshot of a filesystem plus metadata (the command to run, environment, ports). Built from a `Dockerfile`. |
| **Container** | A **running (or stopped) instance** of an image. You can start many containers from one image. |
| **Registry** | A store of images (e.g. Docker Hub). `docker pull` downloads; `docker push` uploads. |

### The commands you'll use constantly

| Command | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `docker run <image>` | Create **and** start a container from an image |
| `docker ps` | List running containers (`-a` includes stopped ones) |
| `docker images` | List local images |
| `docker pull <image>` | Download an image from a registry |
| `docker exec -it <c> sh` | Run a command (e.g. a shell) **inside** a running container |
| `docker stop` / `docker rm` | Stop a container / remove it |
| `docker build -t name:tag .` | Build an image from a `Dockerfile` |

### Mental model

> **Image is to container what a class is to an object.** One image (the template) → many containers (the instances).

The terminal below talks to a Docker daemon running inside this lab. When you're comfortable with the vocabulary, mark this lesson complete and try the exercises.
